answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting work vs. higher salary + management opportunity.” The short version is:

> **Intrinsic features of work—interesting tasks, autonomy, mastery, meaning, good colleagues, and growth—are usually stronger predictors of job satisfaction than pay, especially once pay is already high.**  
> But: if “leading a team” is itself deeply motivating to you, that may count as an intrinsic feature, not merely an extrinsic promotion.

Below are the most relevant findings.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A large literature on the **Job Characteristics Model** studies which properties of work make people satisfied and motivated. The classic dimensions are:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- meaningfulness of the work

Meta-analyses generally find that these features correlate substantially with job satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower turnover intentions.

Important references:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976** — Job Characteristics Model  
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** — meta-analysis of the model  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** — large meta-analysis of work design

In Humphrey et al. 2007, work characteristics like autonomy, task significance, task variety, feedback, and social support showed moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction. In plain English: people tend to be happier in jobs where the work itself is engaging, meaningful, and gives them agency.

For your case, “very interesting research engineering work” likely maps onto several of these: skill variety, autonomy, mastery, novelty, task significance, and identity.

---

## 2. Pay matters, but its effect on job satisfaction is surprisingly modest

Pay is not irrelevant. But once people are financially secure, additional pay tends to have a weaker effect than people expect.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”

They found that actual pay level had only a modest relationship with job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .15** with overall job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction is more related to pay, unsurprisingly, but overall job satisfaction is much more strongly tied to the content and context of the work.

Related broader well-being research:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that income improves life evaluation, but emotional well-being showed diminishing returns above a certain level.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the strict “plateau” idea, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled the findings: income keeps helping for many people, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and varies by baseline happiness.

The practical implication: if you are already highly paid at Google, a “somewhat higher” salary at Meta may improve financial satisfaction, but it is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day interest unless the money changes something major: financial independence timeline, family security, ability to buy a home, etc.

---

## 3. Intrinsic motivation predicts well-being and often performance

Self-Determination Theory is one of the most influential frameworks here. It says people thrive when work satisfies three needs:

- **autonomy** — I have agency  
- **competence** — I am growing and using my abilities  
- **relatedness** — I feel connected to others

Relevant references:

- **Deci & Ryan, 1985 / 2000** — Self-Determination Theory  
- **Van den Broeck et al., 2016** — review/meta-analysis of psychological needs at work  
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014** — meta-analysis on intrinsic motivation and incentives

The consistent finding: intrinsic motivation is strongly associated with engagement, satisfaction, persistence, and well-being. Extrinsic incentives like pay and status help, but they are usually not enough if the work itself becomes dull, low-autonomy, or misaligned.

For you, the key question is whether the Meta job is “less interesting technically” but still intrinsically motivating because of leadership, ownership, and impact — or whether it is genuinely less meaningful day-to-day.

---

## 4. “Meaningful work” has a very strong relationship with job satisfaction

Interesting work and meaningful work are not identical, but they often overlap. A large meta-analysis:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019**, “Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis”

They found meaningful work was strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

So if your current Google role feels not just intellectually interesting but also meaningful, that is a major point in its favor. If the Meta role is less intellectually interesting but gives you more impact through leading people, mentoring, and shaping direction, it may still be meaningful in a different way.

---

## 5. Person–job fit matters: people are happier when work matches their interests

Vocational psychology has studied “interest fit” for decades: people tend to be more satisfied and perform better when their work matches their interests and identity.

Relevant references:

- **Holland, 1997** — vocational interest theory  
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012** — meta-analysis of vocational interests  
- **Van Iddekinge et al., 2011** — interest congruence and work outcomes

The effects are usually moderate rather than enormous, but reliable. People whose jobs fit their interests tend to report higher satisfaction and somewhat better performance/retention.

In your case, if your identity is strongly “research engineer working on intellectually deep problems,” giving that up may be costly. If your emerging identity is “technical leader / manager / builder of teams,” then the Meta offer may fit you better than the surface description suggests.

---

## 6. Promotion and leadership are mixed: more autonomy and status, but also more stress

The evidence on promotions is not simply “promotion makes people happier.”

Promotions can increase:

- income  
- status  
- autonomy  
- influence  
- perceived career progress

But they can also increase:

- hours  
- emotional labor  
- responsibility for others’ problems  
- conflict  
- administrative work  
- distance from hands-on technical work

Some studies find promotions increase job satisfaction, at least in the short term, but the effect on broader life satisfaction and mental health is more mixed.

Relevant work:

- **Kosteas, 2011** — promotions and job satisfaction  
- **Johnston & Lee, 2013** — effects of promotions on well-being  
- **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** — promotion and mental strain/health-related outcomes

For your situation, “leading a small team” could be a big positive if it satisfies a long-standing intrinsic desire. But it should not be treated automatically as “better.” Management is a different job. If what you love is research depth, the management component may eventually feel like overhead. If what you love is multiplying impact through people, it may be energizing.

---

## 7. Regret research: direct evidence is weaker, but missed opportunities matter

There is less direct research on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying one?” But general regret research has some relevant findings.

Classic results:

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** — in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** — major life regrets often cluster around education, career, romance, and missed opportunities.
- **Morrison & Roese, 2011** — career and education are among common domains of regret.

This suggests a subtle point: if leading a team is something you have always wanted to try, not trying it could become a source of regret. But that does not mean the Meta offer is automatically right. It means the “leadership opportunity” deserves serious weight as an intrinsic/growth factor, not just as status.

A useful question is:

> Would I regret not becoming a team lead more than I would regret spending several years on less interesting work?

That is closer to what the regret literature can illuminate.

---

## 8. My evidence-based read of your tradeoff

Based on the research, I would not treat “higher salary” as the main reason to move if you are already well-compensated. The evidence suggests salary has relatively modest marginal effects compared with the daily quality of the work.

The real tradeoff is probably:

### Google role
Pros:
- highly interesting work  
- likely strong intrinsic motivation  
- possible identity fit as research engineer  
- probably high autonomy/mastery

Cons:
- maybe less leadership opportunity  
- possible regret if you want to manage/lead  
- career growth path may be less aligned with your long-term goals

### Meta role
Pros:
- team leadership  
- higher salary  
- growth into management/technical leadership  
- more influence over people/project direction

Cons:
- less interesting domain  
- possible reduction in technical depth  
- more meetings/coordination/conflict  
- risk that the leadership title does not compensate for less engaging work

Research would say: **do not underrate the interestingness of the actual work.** It is one of the central drivers of job satisfaction. But also: **if leadership is itself deeply interesting to you**, then the Meta job may be intrinsically attractive in a different dimension.

---

## 9. Questions I would use to apply the research

To make the decision more empirical, I’d score both roles on these dimensions:

1. **Autonomy**: How much control will I have over technical/product direction?  
2. **Mastery**: Will I keep learning at a high rate?  
3. **Meaning/impact**: Do I care about the work’s purpose?  
4. **Interest**: Will I enjoy an average Tuesday?  
5. **Relatedness**: Do I like/respect the people and manager?  
6. **Leadership growth**: Will I actually lead, or just coordinate?  
7. **Reversibility**: If I dislike management, can I return to IC/research?  
8. **Financial significance**: Is the salary increase life-changing or merely nice?  
9. **Career capital**: Which option gives me more future optionality?  
10. **Regret minimization**: Which path would I be more upset never having tried?

If the Meta role scores high on autonomy, leadership growth, team quality, and reversibility, it may be worth serious consideration despite less interesting subject matter. If it is mainly “more money + title, but boring work,” the research would generally favor staying with the more interesting job.

answer:
The research base is not perfect for your exact question—there are no clean randomized studies of “stay at Google doing fascinating research vs. join Meta for more money and a management role.” But organizational psychology and happiness economics have fairly consistent findings.

## Short version

Across many studies, **intrinsic job qualities**—interesting work, autonomy, skill use, learning, task significance, good colleagues/manager—are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and well-being than pay level**, especially once pay is already high enough to remove financial stress.

A salary increase is not irrelevant, but for someone already at Google/Meta compensation levels, the expected well-being gain from “a bit higher salary” is likely modest unless it materially changes your life: financial independence timeline, housing, family constraints, risk tolerance, etc.

The harder part is that “leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsic reward if you genuinely want to manage, coach, set direction, and have broader impact. So the comparison may not be:

> interesting work vs. salary

but rather:

> deep technical/research interest vs. leadership/autonomy/impact/growth, plus somewhat more money, minus less interesting technical content.

## 1. Job satisfaction is meaningfully related to overall life satisfaction

Work is a large part of waking life, so job quality matters.

A classic meta-analysis by **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** found a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, around **r ≈ .4**. Later work generally supports a reciprocal relationship: happier people tend to be more satisfied at work, but satisfying work also contributes to overall well-being.

So this is not a trivial “career-only” question. If one job would make your daily work much less engaging, that can matter for life satisfaction.

## 2. “Interesting work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under constructs like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- complexity,
- meaningful work,
- person–job fit.

The **Job Characteristics Model** by **Hackman & Oldham** and later meta-analyses find that these intrinsic characteristics are strongly related to job satisfaction and motivation.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** found that work-design variables such as autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and social support tend to correlate with job satisfaction in the rough range of **.3 to .5**, sometimes higher depending on the variable. These are not tiny effects.

Cross-national survey research also tends to find that “interesting work,” good relationships with management, autonomy, and job security are among the most robust predictors of job satisfaction. Pay matters, but it is usually not the dominant factor once basic needs are met.

## 3. Pay matters, but its direct link to job satisfaction is modest

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, on pay and job satisfaction. They found that actual pay level had only a modest association with job satisfaction, roughly **r ≈ .15**, and a somewhat larger but still moderate association with pay satisfaction.

This does not mean money is unimportant. Money can matter a lot when it changes constraints: debt, housing, family planning, immigration risk, ability to retire early, ability to take future risks, etc. But as a direct driver of daily happiness, especially at already-high income, pay is usually weaker than job content, autonomy, manager quality, work hours, and stress.

Happiness-economics research similarly finds that income is related to well-being, often roughly logarithmically: each proportional increase matters less than the previous one. **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being plateaued around a certain income level, though later work by **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** complicates that picture and suggests well-being can keep rising with income for many people. Still, the practical point remains: a modest raise at a very high baseline salary is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day job quality.

## 4. Person–job fit may be the most relevant concept

For your case, the key question may be less “interesting vs. money” and more “fit.”

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, with correlations often around **.5**. That is large by social-science standards.

So ask: which role fits your motives better?

If your core motive is intellectual depth, technical novelty, and doing frontier research, the Google role may fit better.

If your core motive is influence, coaching people, setting direction, being accountable for outcomes, and building leadership skill, the Meta role may fit better—even if the technical work is less fascinating.

But it is important to distinguish two versions of “I always wanted to lead a small team”:

1. **Intrinsic leadership interest**: “I enjoy mentoring, setting strategy, handling ambiguity, helping others grow, making tradeoffs, and being responsible for a group outcome.”
2. **Symbolic/status interest**: “I like the idea of being a manager/lead, but I may not enjoy the actual calendar: meetings, performance management, prioritization, conflict, less hands-on research.”

The research would predict better outcomes for case 1 than case 2.

## 5. Management can improve well-being through autonomy and impact, but can hurt it through demands

Leadership roles often provide more autonomy, influence, task significance, and career growth. Those are positive predictors of satisfaction.

But they also often increase:

- interruptions,
- meetings,
- people problems,
- responsibility without control,
- political work,
- performance pressure,
- less time for deep individual contribution.

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature and **Karasek’s demand-control model** suggest that demanding jobs are more tolerable, and sometimes energizing, when they also provide high control, support, and resources. But high demand plus low control/support is a classic recipe for strain.

So a small-team lead role could be very satisfying if it gives you real autonomy and a good team. It could be unpleasant if you inherit unclear goals, low-quality management above you, political constraints, or a lot of administrative burden.

## 6. Regret research gives weaker, but relevant, evidence

There is less direct research on “people regret choosing interesting work over higher pay” or vice versa.

General regret research does find that career and education are among the most common life-regret domains. **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education, career, and romance are among the areas people most often regret.

A related finding from **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** is that short-term regrets are often about actions taken, while long-term regrets are often about inactions—things people did not try. That could matter here: if leading a team is a personally important untested goal, declining the opportunity may have some long-term regret risk.

But this does not imply you should take it. The regret literature would instead suggest asking:

- “Will I regret not trying leadership?”
- “Will I regret leaving unusually interesting research?”
- “Which option is more reversible?”
- “Which option better matches the kind of person I want to become?”

Reversibility is important. If moving to Meta gives you management experience but makes it harder to return to deep research, that is one kind of option cost. If staying at Google preserves research depth but delays your transition to leadership, that is another.

## 7. Affective forecasting research: focus on a typical Tuesday

Another relevant literature is affective forecasting. People often overestimate how much big, salient changes will affect happiness and underestimate the importance of repeated daily experiences. **Kahneman et al.** call this related mistake the “focusing illusion”: when thinking about one salient factor, we exaggerate its importance.

For job decisions, this means salary, company name, and title are very salient. But your experienced well-being will be driven heavily by things like:

- What do you do from 10am to 12pm on a normal Tuesday?
- How many meetings?
- Do you respect your manager?
- Are your teammates strong?
- Do you have autonomy?
- Are you learning?
- Are you proud of the work?
- Are you bored?
- Are you stressed in a good way or a corrosive way?

The research strongly suggests that these repeated daily factors matter more than people often predict.

## Evidence-weighted interpretation for your case

Based on the literature, I would treat the factors roughly like this:

| Factor | Research implication |
|---|---|
| More interesting work | Strong positive predictor of job satisfaction, engagement, and well-being |
| Slightly higher salary | Positive, but probably modest at your compensation level unless it changes life constraints |
| Leading a team | Potentially very positive if it satisfies autonomy, competence, impact, and identity; negative if it mainly means admin/stress |
| Less interesting technical content | Real risk; do not dismiss it as a luxury |
| Manager/team quality | Very important; should be heavily investigated |
| Workload/stress/control | Important; high demands are okay mainly if paired with control/support |
| Fit with future self | Highly relevant; person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors |

## Practical research-based heuristic

If the Meta role is only “less interesting work + somewhat more money + management title,” the research would make me skeptical that the move improves happiness.

If the Meta role is “less technically fascinating, but genuinely meaningful leadership, high autonomy, strong team, good manager, clear scope, and a chance to build a version of yourself you care about,” then the research case becomes much stronger.

The main thing I would not do is let the salary bump dominate the decision. The literature is pretty clear that, especially at high income, **daily work quality and fit usually matter more than a modest pay increase**.

answer:
Yes—there is a fairly large literature, though it usually studies “interesting work” under labels like **intrinsic motivation, autonomy, skill variety, task significance, meaningful work, person–job fit, and job complexity** rather than “interestingness” directly.

## Short version of the evidence

1. **The content of the work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**  
   Jobs with autonomy, challenge, skill use, variety, feedback, and meaning reliably predict higher job satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover intentions.

2. **Pay matters, but its association with job satisfaction is usually weaker than people expect, especially once income is already high.**  
   Meta-analyses find only modest correlations between pay level and job/pay satisfaction. Income still matters for security, options, status, and fairness, but marginal well-being gains diminish.

3. **Leadership/management can increase satisfaction if it gives autonomy, impact, growth, and identity fit—but can reduce satisfaction if it replaces intrinsically enjoyable technical work with meetings, politics, coordination, and stress.**  
   The key variable is not “manager vs IC” but whether the new role satisfies the same motivational needs.

4. **Direct research on “will I regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/managerial job?” is limited.**  
   But general regret research suggests people often regret *inaction* and failing to pursue personally important aspirations. So if “leading a team” is a long-standing ideal, that matters.

---

## 1. Interesting work strongly predicts job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. It argues that people are more satisfied and motivated when jobs provide:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- meaningfulness,
- responsibility.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** found that motivational work characteristics—especially autonomy, task significance, feedback, and job complexity—are strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction,
- internal work motivation,
- organizational commitment,
- lower stress,
- lower turnover intentions,
- performance.

The correlations are not tiny; many are in the roughly **.25–.45** range depending on the specific job feature and outcome. In organizational psychology, those are meaningful effects.

So the answer to “do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier at work?” is broadly: **yes**. More intrinsically motivating work is one of the most consistent predictors of job satisfaction.

A related literature on **person–job fit** and **vocational interest fit** also finds that people are more satisfied and more likely to persist when their work matches their interests. Meta-analyses generally find modest-to-moderate relationships, often around **.15–.25** for interest congruence and satisfaction/persistence. Not enormous, but real and robust.

---

## 2. Job satisfaction is related to life satisfaction, not just “work mood”

You asked about happiness, not only job satisfaction. The two are meaningfully connected.

Meta-analyses such as **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** and later work by **Bowling et al.** find a moderate relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often around **.30–.40**. Causality is bidirectional: happier people tend to be more satisfied with jobs, but job quality also affects overall well-being.

This matters because work takes a large fraction of waking life. If one job is substantially more engaging day-to-day, that tends to show up not only in workplace attitudes but in broader well-being.

---

## 3. Pay has weaker links to job satisfaction than intrinsic work factors

There is a useful meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** on pay and job satisfaction. They found that actual pay level is only modestly related to:

- job satisfaction: about **r ≈ .15**,
- pay satisfaction: about **r ≈ .20–.25**.

That does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It means that once you compare across people and jobs, pay explains only a limited amount of variation in satisfaction.

On general well-being, income does predict life evaluation and experienced well-being, but with diminishing marginal returns. Relevant findings:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, famously around $75k in their US data, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically, without a sharp plateau.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: reconciled some of the disagreement: for many people, well-being keeps rising with income, but for the least happy group, it may flatten after a certain level.

For someone already at a Google Research Engineer compensation level, the implication is: **a somewhat higher salary may improve options and satisfaction, but the expected marginal happiness gain is probably much smaller than the effect of a large change in daily work quality.**

If the salary difference is life-changing—e.g., changes housing, family security, immigration risk, retirement timeline—that is different. But if it is “nice but not transformative,” the literature would not predict it to dominate intrinsic job quality.

---

## 4. Meaningful and intrinsically motivating work is especially important in creative/research roles

For research/engineering/creative work, intrinsic motivation is particularly relevant.

**Teresa Amabile’s** research on creativity found that people are more creative when they are intrinsically motivated—when they find the work itself interesting, challenging, and meaningful. Her work on the “progress principle” also suggests that making progress on meaningful work is one of the strongest drivers of positive inner work life.

Self-determination theory—**Deci & Ryan**—also finds that people thrive when work satisfies needs for:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness.

A meta-analysis by **Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999** found that controlling extrinsic rewards can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This does not mean “higher salary is bad”; salary can also signal fairness and competence. But it does suggest that for intrinsically motivated technical/research work, replacing interesting problems with external rewards alone is risky.

For someone in research engineering, “interestingness” is not a luxury variable. It may be tied to motivation, learning, creativity, and long-term career capital.

---

## 5. But leadership is not just an extrinsic reward

The Meta offer has two different components:

1. **higher salary**, and  
2. **leading a small team**, which you say you have always wanted.

The research would treat those differently.

The salary increase is mostly extrinsic. But leading a team could satisfy intrinsic needs:

- autonomy,
- impact,
- mentoring,
- status/recognition,
- skill growth,
- identity development,
- broader scope.

So the real comparison is not simply:

> interesting work vs money

It is more like:

> current intrinsic technical interest  
> vs  
> less interesting domain but more leadership, scope, and career development.

Leadership can be highly motivating if it gives you more agency and impact. But it can also reduce satisfaction if the daily work becomes mostly coordination, performance management, meetings, hiring, prioritization, and organizational negotiation.

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature, associated with **Bakker & Demerouti**, is relevant here. Jobs with more responsibility often bring both:

- more resources: autonomy, influence, recognition, learning;
- more demands: stress, ambiguity, emotional labor, accountability, interruptions.

Whether the net effect is positive depends heavily on fit.

---

## 6. Regret research: limited but relevant

There is not much clean research following people who chose “interesting job” vs “higher-paying/managerial job” and measuring regret years later. But broader regret research has some useful findings.

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that in the short term people often regret actions, but in the long term they more often regret **inactions**—things they did not try.

**Morrison & Roese, 2011** found that common life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting. Career regrets are a major category.

**Davidai & Gilovich, 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets often involve failures to live up to their “ideal self”—their hopes, aspirations, and dreams—more than failures of duty.

Applied cautiously to your case: if “I always wanted to lead a team” is part of your ideal professional self, not merely a title/status desire, then ignoring it could become a regret risk. But if the real day-to-day of the Meta role is not the kind of leadership you imagine, then taking it could also produce regret.

So regret research does **not** say “take the offer.” It says: pay attention to the thing you have repeatedly wanted to try, because untried aspirations are a common source of long-term regret.

---

## 7. My research-based interpretation

If the Meta role is **less interesting only technically**, but gives you meaningful leadership, autonomy, impact, and growth, then the research does not clearly favor staying. The new role may satisfy different intrinsic motives.

If the Meta role is **less interesting overall**—less autonomy, less meaningful problems, less learning, more bureaucracy—and the main upsides are salary and title, then the literature would generally predict lower long-term satisfaction despite the pay increase, especially at already-high compensation levels.

The most important question is probably:

> Is “leading a small team” itself interesting and meaningful enough to compensate for less interesting technical work?

If yes, the Meta role may be a good fit.  
If no, the higher salary alone is unlikely to compensate for a major drop in intrinsic work quality.

---

## A useful way to evaluate the two jobs

From the research, I would compare them on these dimensions:

| Factor | Evidence-based importance |
|---|---|
| Daily intrinsic interest | Very high predictor of job satisfaction |
| Autonomy/control | Very high |
| Skill growth | High |
| Meaning/impact | High |
| Quality of manager/org environment | High |
| Leadership opportunity | Potentially high if it matches your goals |
| Salary increase | Important, but usually weaker once already financially secure |
| Status/title | Can matter, but often adapts quickly |
| Reversibility | Important for regret/option value |
| Stress/time cost | Very important, often underestimated |

A particularly useful question is:

> If salary were equal, would I still seriously consider the Meta role because of the leadership opportunity?

If yes, the offer is not just about money.  
If no, the evidence would make me skeptical that the salary bump alone will compensate for substantially less interesting work.

answer:
Yes—there is a lot of relevant research, though it is usually framed as **intrinsic motivation, job design, person–job fit, meaningful work, autonomy, skill use**, etc., rather than simply “interesting work.”

## Short version

The research generally says:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its marginal effect on happiness/job satisfaction is usually much smaller once income is already high/adequate.**
3. **Leadership/promotion can increase satisfaction/status/autonomy, but also increases stress and may reduce time spent on intrinsically enjoyable work.**
4. **Regret research suggests people often regret not pursuing important opportunities, especially “ideal self” goals—so your long-standing desire to lead is relevant.**
5. In your specific situation—Google/Meta, already high compensation, “a bit higher salary” vs “much less interesting work”—the evidence would usually put substantial weight on the day-to-day interestingness/fit of the work, unless the leadership role itself is deeply motivating to you.

---

## 1. Intrinsic job characteristics strongly predict job satisfaction

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. It argues that people are more motivated and satisfied when work has:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

Later meta-analyses broadly support this.

A major meta-analysis:

> **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
> Work design features such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity were consistently related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

The exact correlations vary by feature and study, but the general pattern is robust: jobs that let people use skills, solve meaningful problems, learn, and exercise autonomy produce higher satisfaction and engagement.

For your case, “interesting work” probably maps onto:

- intellectual challenge,
- novelty,
- problem-solving,
- autonomy,
- using your strongest skills,
- being around strong researchers/engineers,
- feeling the work matters.

Those are not minor factors in the literature; they are central.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is a very strong predictor

Another relevant body of research is **person–job fit**: how well the job matches your interests, abilities, and needs.

A well-known meta-analysis:

> **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, *Personnel Psychology*  
> Person–job fit was strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intention to quit. The correlation with job satisfaction was around the mid-.50s in many estimates, which is large for organizational psychology.

This matters because your question is not merely “interesting vs boring.” It is closer to:

> “Does this role fit the kind of work that gives me energy and uses my best abilities?”

If Google gives you unusually good person–job fit, that is a serious asset. A modest pay increase elsewhere may not compensate for a large fit decrease.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is highly associated with well-being

There is also research on **meaningful work**. Meaningful work overlaps with interesting work but is not identical. It includes feeling that the work matters, expresses your values, or contributes to something important.

Meta-analytic work, e.g.:

> **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
> Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction and work engagement, and moderately associated with overall life satisfaction and lower withdrawal intentions.

In many studies, meaningfulness and intrinsic motivation are among the stronger predictors of whether people like their jobs.

So if your current Google work feels not just technically interesting but also meaningful or identity-relevant—“this is the kind of work I want to be doing”—that is important.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but often less than people expect at high income levels

There is clear evidence that pay matters. It affects security, options, perceived fairness, status, and life evaluation.

But the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is surprisingly modest.

A useful meta-analysis:

> **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
> Pay level had only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. The reported correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was roughly around **r = .15**, while pay level correlated more strongly with satisfaction with pay itself.

In plain English: higher-paid people are somewhat more satisfied, but salary is far from the dominant driver of job satisfaction.

For overall happiness, income research is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income but appeared to plateau around a certain level in the U.S. sample.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged that, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with log income even at higher levels.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps increasing with income, but the effect is smaller/different across happiness levels.

The practical implication is not “money doesn’t matter.” It is:

> At already-high compensation levels, a modest salary increase is usually less predictive of daily happiness than work content, autonomy, fit, stress, relationships, and meaning.

For someone choosing between two low-paying jobs, money may dominate. For someone choosing between Google and Meta compensation packages, the marginal happiness value of “a bit higher salary” may be limited unless it meaningfully changes your financial trajectory, family constraints, visa/security situation, or ability to retire/change paths.

---

## 5. Leadership/promotion: positive, but mixed

Your Meta offer is not just “more money”; it includes **leading a small team**, which you say you have always wanted. That is important.

Promotion/leadership can increase:

- autonomy,
- status,
- influence,
- learning,
- sense of impact,
- career capital.

Some studies find promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction. For example:

> **Kosteas, 2011**, *Industrial Relations*  
> Promotions and expected promotions were associated with higher job satisfaction.

But leadership also often brings:

- more meetings,
- people problems,
- performance management,
- less deep technical work,
- more responsibility without full control,
- political/organizational stress.

Other research finds promotion can increase strain:

> **Johnston & Lee, 2013**, “Extra status and extra stress”  
> Promotion was associated with increased job demands/stress, with limited evidence of health benefits.

So the research does not say “promotion is good” or “promotion is bad.” It says the effect depends heavily on whether the new role improves the psychological ingredients of good work: autonomy, competence, relatedness, purpose, and fit.

For you, the key question is:

> Is leading a small team itself interesting work to you, or is it mostly a title/status/career milestone attached to less interesting substance?

If leadership is intrinsically motivating—mentoring people, setting direction, multiplying impact, building systems—then Meta may be more interesting than it first appears. If leadership mainly means meetings around a less compelling product/research area, then the intrinsic-cost warning is real.

---

## 6. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are connected

Job satisfaction is not isolated from overall happiness.

Meta-analytic evidence, e.g.:

> **Bowling et al., 2010**, meta-analysis  
> Job satisfaction and subjective well-being/life satisfaction are moderately to strongly related, often around the **.4–.5** range depending on measures.

The direction is bidirectional: happier people tend to perceive work more positively, but work conditions also affect well-being.

Given how much time work occupies, a less interesting job can materially affect life satisfaction—not just career satisfaction.

---

## 7. Regret research: this cuts both ways

There is less direct research of the form “do people regret choosing interesting work over money?” But regret psychology has relevant findings.

Classic work:

> **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**  
> People often regret actions more in the short term, but inactions more in the long term.

And:

> **Roese & Summerville, 2005**  
> The biggest life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and missed opportunities.

More recent work on “ideal self” regrets also suggests people especially regret not pursuing goals tied to who they wanted to become.

This matters because you said:

> “Leading a small team is something I always wanted to do.”

That creates a possible long-term regret risk if you never try leadership. But the opposite regret is also possible: taking a less interesting job for a title/salary and then missing deeply engaging research work.

So the regret literature would not give a simple answer. It would suggest asking:

- Is leadership part of my “ideal self,” or just a career checkbox?
- Is this Meta opportunity rare, or can I get leadership at Google/elsewhere later?
- Is the interesting research at Google unusually rare?
- Which loss would feel more like betraying my long-term identity?

---

## Evidence-based interpretation for your case

Based on the research, I would summarize it this way:

### If the Meta offer is mainly:
- slightly more salary,
- management title,
- but substantially less interesting day-to-day work,

then the literature suggests caution. Pay/status often do not compensate well for poor intrinsic fit, especially at high income levels.

### If the Meta offer gives you:
- real autonomy,
- a chance to shape direction,
- meaningful leadership,
- strong people to mentor,
- broader impact,
- a path into work you may find interesting in a different way,

then the “less interesting” technical content may be offset by a new source of intrinsic motivation: leadership itself.

The crucial distinction is:

> Are you trading interesting research for extrinsic rewards, or trading one kind of interesting work for another kind—technical depth for leadership/impact?

The research is much more favorable to the second trade than the first.

---

## A practical research-aligned way to decide

Rate each role, not on prestige, but on the variables the literature repeatedly finds important:

| Factor | Google current role | Meta offer |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual interest | ? | ? |
| Autonomy/control | ? | ? |
| Skill growth | ? | ? |
| Meaning/impact | ? | ? |
| Quality of colleagues | ? | ? |
| Manager/org support | ? | ? |
| Stress/workload | ? | ? |
| Leadership development | ? | ? |
| Compensation impact after tax | ? | ? |
| Future optionality | ? | ? |

The research would generally weight the first seven quite heavily, especially if compensation is already high.

I would also ask Meta very concrete questions:

- How much time would I spend coding/researching vs meetings?
- What decisions would I actually own?
- How strong is the team?
- What are the success metrics?
- Is the area strategically stable?
- Can I shape the technical agenda?
- Is this a people-management role, tech-lead role, or hybrid?
- What happens if I want to return to a more research-heavy role?

And ask internally at Google:

- Is there a realistic path to leading a small team here?
- Could I get tech-lead/managerial responsibility without giving up the interesting domain?
- Is there an internal transfer or project that gives me both?

---

## Bottom line

The research does not say “always choose interesting work.” But it does say that **intrinsic work quality—interest, autonomy, fit, meaning, skill use—is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction**, while **salary has a weaker marginal relationship once you are already well compensated**.

So, if Meta is genuinely much less interesting and only modestly better paid, the evidence leans toward being careful about leaving highly engaging work.

But if leading a team is itself a deep intrinsic goal for you, then the right comparison is not “interesting work vs money.” It is:

> “Current technical interestingness at Google” vs “leadership/autonomy/impact growth at Meta.”

That is the real tradeoff the research would suggest you analyze.

answer:
There is research, but not usually in the exact form “interesting research job vs. less-interesting management job with higher pay.” The closest literatures are job design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, meaningful work, pay/satisfaction, and regret/affective forecasting.

Short version: **intrinsically interesting, autonomous, meaningful work is one of the more robust predictors of job satisfaction and engagement. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases—especially at already-high income levels—usually predict much smaller changes in happiness than job content, autonomy, fit, manager/team quality, and work-life conditions.** The “leading a small team” part is also a real positive factor if leadership itself is interesting to you; it can satisfy autonomy/competence/status/growth needs, but it can also replace deep work with coordination stress.

## 1. Interesting/autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction

A major framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they have autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback.

Relevant evidence:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976; Fried & Ferris, 1987; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**: meta-analyses and reviews find that motivational job characteristics—especially **autonomy**, **task variety**, **task significance**, and **feedback**—are consistently associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower turnover intentions.
- In these meta-analyses, correlations between core job characteristics and job satisfaction are often in the **small-to-moderate to large range**, roughly around **r = .25–.50**, depending on the characteristic and outcome.

For your case, “very interesting stuff” likely maps onto several of these: skill variety, learning, autonomy, intellectual challenge, task significance, and feedback from hard technical progress.

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another strong finding: people are happier when their job fits their interests, values, and preferred work style.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, a large meta-analysis of person–environment fit, found that **person–job fit** correlated strongly with:
  - job satisfaction: about **r = .56**
  - organizational commitment: about **r = .47**
  - lower turnover intentions: about **r = –.46**
  - performance: smaller but positive, around **r = .20**

This is highly relevant. If you are unusually motivated by deep technical/research work, a less intellectually interesting role may be costly even if it is objectively “better” on salary/title.

But the reverse can also be true: if leading people is a core unrealized interest for you, then the Meta role may fit a different part of your preferences.

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research/engineering they often overlap.

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work, found meaningful work was strongly related to:
  - job satisfaction, roughly **r ≈ .60**
  - work engagement, roughly **r ≈ .50–.60**
  - organizational commitment
  - lower burnout / distress
  - life satisfaction, with smaller but still positive associations

So people who experience their work as meaningful or intrinsically valuable do tend to report better work outcomes and somewhat better general well-being.

Caveat: much of this is correlational. Happier people may also perceive their work as more meaningful. But longitudinal and job-design evidence generally supports the idea that job characteristics themselves matter, not only personality.

## 4. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually weaker than people expect

Pay is important, especially for financial security, fairness, family obligations, status, and optionality. But the research usually finds that **pay level is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction**, especially once basic comfort/security is met.

A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al., 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction,” found:
  - pay level vs. job satisfaction: about **r = .15**
  - pay level vs. pay satisfaction: about **r = .23**

So pay predicts satisfaction, but not nearly as strongly as person–job fit, autonomy, meaningfulness, or job design.

On general happiness:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**, and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** found a more nuanced pattern: experienced well-being generally continues to rise with income, but roughly logarithmically, meaning a percentage increase matters more than an absolute increase, and each additional dollar has diminishing impact.

For someone already at Google/Meta compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” may improve satisfaction somewhat, but research would not predict it to dominate daily work quality unless the money materially changes your life plans, risk tolerance, family situation, or sense of fairness.

## 5. Leadership/advancement can be satisfying, but it changes the job

The Meta offer has a different non-monetary advantage: **leading a small team**. That is not just “title”; it can give autonomy, influence, mentoring opportunities, and career capital.

Research-adjacent points:

- Promotions and increased responsibility are often associated with higher job satisfaction, especially when they bring autonomy and perceived progress.
- But management also increases demands: meetings, conflict resolution, performance management, hiring, politics, ambiguity, and reduced maker time.
- In the **job demands–resources** literature, responsibility can be a “challenge demand”: motivating for some people, stressful for others. Challenge demands can increase engagement but also strain.

So the key empirical question is not “management good or bad?” It is: **would managing this particular team give you autonomy, competence, relatedness, impact, and learning—or would it mostly reduce the interesting parts of your work?**

## 6. People often overweight easily comparable factors like salary/title

There is also relevant research from judgment and decision-making.

When comparing two jobs side by side, people tend to overweight features that are easy to compare: salary, level, title, company prestige. But after choosing, happiness is often driven by experienced daily reality: tasks, autonomy, manager, team, stress, commute, meetings, and meaning.

Relevant concepts/studies:

- **Hsee & Zhang, 2004**, “distinction bias”: in joint evaluation, people overweight differences that will matter less in separate/experienced evaluation.
- **Kahneman et al.**, focusing illusion: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.” People overestimate the long-term effect of salient differences.
- Affective forecasting research generally shows people are imperfect at predicting adaptation to changes in income/status and the daily impact of work conditions.

This does not mean “ignore salary/title.” It means: be careful that the higher salary and leadership label do not dominate your estimate of what your Tuesdays and Wednesdays will feel like.

## 7. Regret evidence is less direct

There is not much clean evidence saying, “People who chose interesting work over higher pay later regretted/did not regret it.” But regret research gives some clues.

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education and career are among the most common life-regret domains.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that over the short term people often regret actions, but over the long term people more often regret inactions—things they did not try.

This cuts both ways for you:

- If leadership is “something I always wanted to do,” not trying it may become an inaction regret.
- If intellectually interesting research is central to your identity, leaving it for a less interesting role may become an action regret.

The regret literature does not identify a universal winner. It suggests paying attention to which unchosen path would feel like a more important unrealized self.

## Practical research-based interpretation

Given the evidence, I would weight the factors roughly like this:

1. **Daily work content / intellectual interest / autonomy**: very important predictor of job satisfaction.
2. **Person–job fit**: extremely important. If you are a deep technical/research person, do not discount this.
3. **Manager/team quality and organizational environment**: very important; often underweighted.
4. **Leadership opportunity**: potentially very valuable if you intrinsically want management and the role gives real authority/growth.
5. **Salary increase**: positive, but likely modest in happiness impact if you are already financially comfortable.
6. **Prestige/title/brand**: can matter for career capital, but often less for lived well-being than expected.

A useful way to frame it:

> The question is not “interesting work vs. more money.”  
> The real question is “which role gives me a better expected 2-year lived experience and career trajectory, including intellectual energy, growth, autonomy, people I work with, and future optionality?”

Before deciding, I would try to quantify the Meta role’s actual content:

- What percentage of time would be technical/research vs. people/process?
- How much decision authority would you really have?
- Is the team working on less interesting problems permanently, or is there room to shape direction?
- Would you be evaluated mainly on team output, product metrics, research quality, infra delivery, or politics?
- Who is your manager, and how much autonomy do they give?
- Is this a stepping stone to more interesting leadership later?
- If you dislike management after 12–18 months, can you return to an IC/research role?

The research-backed prior is: **do not underestimate interesting work.** It is not a luxury variable; it is one of the strongest predictors of work satisfaction. But if leading a team is itself deeply interesting to you, then the Meta role may not be “less interesting”—it may simply be interesting along a different dimension.

answer:
There is quite a lot of research, though not usually phrased as “interesting work vs. 15% more salary.” The closest literatures are job design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, pay/job satisfaction, and subjective well-being.

## Short version

Across many studies, **intrinsically motivating work** — work that uses your skills, gives autonomy, has variety/learning, feels meaningful, and fits your interests — is one of the strongest predictors of **job satisfaction, engagement, lower burnout, and lower intent to quit**.

**Pay matters**, but in the evidence it is usually a **weaker predictor of job satisfaction than job content**, especially once basic financial needs are comfortably met. Income continues to predict life satisfaction, but mostly in a **logarithmic / diminishing-returns** way. A modest raise at already-high tech compensation is unlikely, on average, to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day work interest.

The “leading a small team” part is not just extrinsic, though. Leadership can increase autonomy, impact, relatedness, status, and learning — all of which are also predictors of satisfaction. So the key research-backed question is less “salary vs. interest” and more:

> Will the Meta role give you more autonomy, mastery, meaning, and growth despite being less technically interesting? Or will your daily work become mostly less-interesting coordination, meetings, and politics?

## 1. Job content is a strong predictor of satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. It says jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** meta-analysis: job characteristics were reliably associated with job satisfaction and internal motivation.  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, a large meta-analysis of work design: motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, skill variety, task significance, and feedback were among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and organizational commitment. Correlations with satisfaction are often in the moderate-to-large range for organizational psychology, roughly around `r ≈ .4` or higher for some dimensions.

In plain language: people tend to be happier in jobs where the work itself is absorbing, skill-using, autonomous, and meaningful.

This maps closely to “interesting work.”

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person–job fit**: how well the job matches your interests, abilities, and preferences.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit correlated strongly with job satisfaction, about `r ≈ .56`, and was also associated with higher commitment and lower intention to quit.

That is a large effect by workplace-research standards. If your current Google role is unusually well matched to your intellectual interests, that is not a trivial factor.

There is also vocational-interest research — for example, work by **Nye, Su, Rounds, Drasgow** and others — showing that people whose interests fit their work environments tend to show better satisfaction, persistence, and performance, though the exact effect sizes vary.

## 3. Intrinsic motivation predicts well-being and performance

Self-determination theory is relevant here. It argues that people do better when work satisfies three needs:

- **autonomy** — I have agency  
- **competence** — I am using and developing skills  
- **relatedness** — I feel connected to others  

At work, these needs are associated with higher engagement and well-being and lower burnout.

Useful reviews/meta-analyses include:

- **Gagné & Deci, 2005** on self-determination theory at work.  
- **Van den Broeck et al., 2016** meta-analysis: satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs at work predicts better well-being, engagement, job satisfaction, and lower burnout.

Your current role may score highly on competence/mastery and interest. The Meta role may score higher on autonomy, impact, and relatedness if leadership is meaningful to you. That is why the leadership element may genuinely matter, not merely as a title.

## 4. Pay matters, but less than people often assume

Pay is not irrelevant. It predicts life satisfaction, reduces stress when money is tight, and signals fairness/status. But the relationship with job satisfaction is weaker than many expect.

A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** examined pay and job satisfaction. Actual pay level had a relatively weak relationship with job satisfaction, around `r ≈ .15`; pay satisfaction was somewhat stronger but still not huge.

Compare that to person–job fit or job characteristics, where correlations with job satisfaction are often much larger.

On general happiness/income:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation continued rising.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, without a sharp plateau.  
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of the disagreement: income continues to matter for many people, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and differs by baseline happiness.

The practical implication: if the Meta offer is only “a bit higher,” and you are already at senior Google compensation, the expected well-being gain from the salary difference alone is probably modest. If the pay difference materially changes your life — house, family security, financial independence timeline — then it matters more.

Also, relative pay and fairness matter. For example:

- **Card et al., 2012** studied salary transparency and found that discovering one was paid below peers reduced satisfaction and increased job-search behavior; discovering one was above peers did not produce a symmetric happiness boost.

So the emotional effect of pay is often more about fairness, comparison, and security than absolute dollars.

## 5. Meaningful work is highly associated with satisfaction

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are different, but often overlap in research roles.

A meta-analysis:

- **Allan et al., 2019** on meaningful work found strong associations between meaningful work and job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

This is relevant if your current Google work feels scientifically or technically meaningful, not just fun. If the Meta work is less interesting but has more impact or leadership meaning, that could partly compensate.

## 6. Evidence from scientists/researchers: people do trade salary for interesting science

One particularly relevant paper is:

- **Stern, 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**

Stern studied R&D labor markets and found evidence that scientists accept lower wages for jobs offering more “science-oriented” attributes — e.g. freedom to publish, scientific environment, intellectually attractive work. The exact monetary estimates depend on the model, but the important result is that researchers appear willing to give up significant compensation for scientific freedom and interesting research content.

That seems directly applicable to someone in a research-engineering role. Among people who strongly value research content, “interesting work” is not a small perk; it is part of the compensation package.

## 7. Regret: evidence is weaker, but there are some patterns

There is less direct evidence like “people who choose interesting work over higher salary regret it less after ten years.” But regret research gives some clues.

Findings:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: education and career are among the most common life-regret domains.  
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: over time, people tend to regret **inactions** more than actions — missed opportunities, things they did not try.  
- Affective-forecasting research by **Gilbert, Wilson** and others suggests people often overestimate the long-term emotional impact of salient changes, such as more money, title, prestige, or a move.

Applied to your case, this cuts both ways:

- If leading a team is something you have “always wanted to do,” not trying it could become an inaction regret.  
- If the less-interesting work means giving up a rare intellectual fit, you could regret losing that too.  
- The research does not say “always choose the interesting job”; it says day-to-day work content and fit are deeply important and often underweighted relative to visible rewards like pay/title.

## 8. A research-backed way to evaluate the two roles

Instead of comparing “Google vs. Meta” or “interesting vs. salary,” compare the roles on variables that the literature repeatedly finds important:

| Factor | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Autonomy | Strong predictor of satisfaction and motivation |
| Skill use / mastery | Central to intrinsic motivation |
| Learning rate | Important for long-term engagement |
| Task significance / impact | Linked to meaningful work |
| Interest fit | Strongly related to job satisfaction |
| Social environment | Relatedness and manager quality matter a lot |
| Workload / stress | High demands without control predict burnout |
| Fairness / compensation | Matters, especially if perceived as unfair |
| Future optionality | Affects regret and career capital |

For each offer, estimate your actual weekly calendar six months in:

- How many hours of deep technical work?  
- How many hours of meetings/status/process?  
- How much freedom to choose technical direction?  
- How good is the manager?  
- How strong is the team?  
- Will you be learning skills you value?  
- Is the Meta team-lead role a real leadership role, or mostly execution management?  
- Could you shape the Meta work to become more interesting?  
- Could you get leadership experience at Google without leaving the interesting work?

## Bottom-line interpretation of the research

The literature would generally support these claims:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, skill-using work is a major determinant of job satisfaction.**  
2. **A modest salary increase is usually a weaker predictor of happiness than job content**, especially at high income levels.  
3. **Leadership can be a genuine source of satisfaction** if it increases autonomy, impact, competence, and relatedness.  
4. **Regret evidence is ambiguous**: you may regret leaving rare interesting work, but you may also regret not trying leadership if it is a core aspiration.  
5. The best predictor is probably your future day-to-day experience, not the offer headline.

So if the Meta role is “less interesting” only technically but gives you meaningful leadership, autonomy, and growth, it could be good. If it is less interesting **and** mostly reduces your autonomy/deep work in exchange for a slightly higher salary and title, the research would lean toward staying with the more intrinsically motivating role.

answer:
Short version: **yes, there is quite a lot of research suggesting that intrinsically interesting/meaningful work, autonomy, and person–job fit are strong predictors of job satisfaction and well-being. Salary matters, but once you are already well-paid, additional salary tends to have a much smaller average relationship with happiness than people often expect.** Evidence on *regret* specifically is weaker, but career regret is often about misfit, missed growth, or choices inconsistent with one’s values.

Below are the most relevant research findings.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

Organizational psychology usually studies “interesting work” under labels such as:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **job complexity/challenge**
- **person–job fit**
- **meaningful work**
- **interest congruence**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. It argues that people are more motivated and satisfied when jobs provide autonomy, feedback, variety, identity, and significance.

Meta-analyses broadly support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** and later **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** found that enriched job characteristics — especially autonomy, skill variety, task significance, and feedback — are consistently associated with higher job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and performance, and lower turnover intentions.
- The associations are usually medium to large by social-science standards. In plain English: work content matters a lot.

This is probably the closest research analogue to “I find the work itself interesting.”

---

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another highly relevant line of research is **person–environment fit**, especially **person–job fit**: whether the work matches your abilities, interests, values, and preferred way of spending time.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction and organizational commitment, and negatively related to intention to quit. The correlation with job satisfaction was roughly in the **.5 range**, which is quite large in this literature.

This is important for your case because “interesting work” may not be universally interesting; it is interesting *to you*. If research problems, technical depth, novelty, and intellectual autonomy are central to your identity, then losing that fit could be costly.

There is also research on **vocational interest congruence** — whether people’s interests match their occupations. Meta-analytic work, e.g. by **Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000** and later research by **Nye and colleagues**, generally finds that interest congruence predicts satisfaction, persistence, and sometimes performance, though effects vary depending on measurement.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research roles.

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al., 2019** on meaningful work found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- organizational commitment
- lower withdrawal intentions
- lower burnout

It is also moderately associated with overall life satisfaction.

One caveat: many studies are correlational. Happier people may perceive work as more meaningful, and meaningful work may also make people happier. But longitudinal and intervention studies generally support the idea that job resources and meaningfulness do influence well-being, not merely reflect it.

---

## 4. Salary matters, but the marginal effect is often smaller than people expect

Salary is not irrelevant. It matters for security, freedom, fairness, status, optionality, family goals, and reduced financial stress.

But the empirical relationship between pay and job satisfaction is surprisingly modest.

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** found that actual pay level had only a weak relationship with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15** — and a somewhat stronger but still modest relationship with pay satisfaction.

So higher-paid people are, on average, somewhat more satisfied, but salary explains much less than work content, fit, autonomy, and meaningfulness.

For general happiness, the income literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps rising with income, but the effect is not huge, and for the least happy group it may flatten at higher incomes.

For someone already working as a research engineer at Google, a “bit higher salary” is likely to have a smaller average effect on daily well-being than a large change in the intrinsic quality of daily work — unless that salary materially changes life constraints.

---

## 5. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

A common misconception is that job satisfaction only affects work life. In fact, job and life satisfaction are moderately correlated.

Meta-analytic work such as **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** and later **Bowling et al., 2010** finds correlations between job satisfaction and life satisfaction around **.4 or higher**.

Causality appears bidirectional: happier people are more satisfied at work, but satisfying work also contributes to overall well-being.

So yes, if the more interesting job makes you substantially more satisfied at work, research suggests that can matter for overall happiness too.

---

## 6. Evidence on regret is weaker

There is less direct research of the form:

> “People who chose higher salary over interesting work later regretted it.”

That exact question is hard to study because people self-select, opportunities differ, and counterfactuals are unobservable.

However, regret research offers some relevant hints:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that career and education are among the most common domains of major life regret.
- Long-term regrets often involve perceived missed opportunities or choices inconsistent with one’s identity or aspirations.
- Affective forecasting research by **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman and others** shows that people often overweight salient features at decision time — salary, title, prestige — and underweight repeated daily experiences.

That last point is relevant: salary and title are very salient when comparing offers; the daily calendar is less salient but often matters more for experienced well-being.

---

## 7. Leadership is not purely an extrinsic factor

In your case, the Meta offer is not just “more money, less interesting work.” It also includes **leading a small team**, which you said you have always wanted.

Research would treat that as potentially important because leadership can increase:

- autonomy
- responsibility
- task significance
- learning
- social impact
- identity fit
- career capital

Those are all intrinsic or semi-intrinsic job characteristics.

But management can also increase:

- meetings
- conflict
- ambiguity
- emotional labor
- less hands-on research/engineering
- responsibility without enough control

So the key research-informed question is not simply:

> “Interesting work vs. higher salary?”

It is more like:

> “Which role gives me the better combination of autonomy, learning, competence, meaning, social impact, and person–job fit?”

If leading people is itself deeply interesting to you, the Meta role may be more intrinsically motivating than it first appears. If you mostly want leadership abstractly — title, progression, status — but dislike the day-to-day management work, the research would predict less benefit.

---

## Practical takeaway from the research

The strongest evidence suggests:

1. **Do not underestimate day-to-day work content.**  
   Autonomy, challenge, fit, and meaningfulness are robust predictors of satisfaction.

2. **Do not overestimate a modest salary increase, especially at already high income.**  
   Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than intrinsic work quality.

3. **Treat leadership as part of the intrinsic-work question.**  
   If managing, mentoring, setting direction, and growing people genuinely excite you, that may compensate for less interesting technical content.

4. **Evaluate the actual weekly calendar, not the job label.**  
   Research on well-being suggests repeated daily experiences drive happiness more than abstract prestige or compensation.

A research-consistent heuristic would be:

> If the salary increase does not materially change your life, and the new role is genuinely much less interesting day to day, staying with the more interesting work is usually better supported by the well-being literature.  
> But if the leadership component is itself a major source of meaning, growth, and identity fit for you, then the new role may satisfy the same psychological needs through a different path.
